# PM-092 — incident.io

**Company:** incident.io  
**Category:** Database  
**Source:** https://incident.io/blog/database-performance

## Incident Summary

Two weeks of intermittent app timeouts, with traces showing requests waiting up to 20s for a connection from Go's `database/sql` pool, but contention spread across many endpoints rather than a single slow query. After 24 deploys' worth of fixes (materialized views, indexes, lock timeouts, async Slack-webhook handling, and a custom `ngrok/sqlmw` middleware to attribute connection-pool hold time per operation), the root cause turned out to be an unnecessary transaction wrapping every Slack modal submission — many small fast transactions were in aggregate exhausting the pool.

# PM-170 — incident.io

**Company:** incident.io  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://incident.io/blog/clouds-caches-and-connection-conundrums

## Incident Summary

After moving to Google Cloud they saw spikes of Postgres connection timeouts (~200 new connections/s) and memcached "i/o timeout" errors. Pool tuning (15m→30m max lifetime, static pool size, `MaxIdleConns` 2→20) helped each in turn but didn't eliminate the cache errors. The smoking gun was GKE Dataplane V2: bursts of parallel outbound calls (made worse by an accidental N+1 join) saturated the per-node `anetd` agent's CPU, dropping packets between the node and other services running on it.

**Service:** PostgreSQL  
**Severity:** High  
**Tags:** database, postgres, connections, pgbouncer

- New queries queue or fail immediately

# PM-153 — Google

**Company:** Google  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://status.cloud.google.com/incidents/X8SNkK2BPyCrc1sveeiu

## Incident Summary

Filestore enforces a global limit on API requests to limit impact in overload scenarios. The outage was triggered when an internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling of the Filestore API. This continued until the internal service was manually paused. As a result of this throttling, read-only API access was unavailable for all customers. This affected customers in all locations, due to a global quota that applies to Filestore. Console, gcloud and API access (List, GetOperation, etc.) calls all failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes. Mutate operations (CreateInstance, UpdateInstance, CreateBackup, etc.) still succeeded, but customers were unable to check on operation progress.

# PM-192 — PagerDuty

**Company:** PagerDuty  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://web.archive.org/web/20211019062735/https://www.pagerduty.com/blog/outage-post-mortem-april-13-2013/

## Incident Summary

In April 2013, PagerDuty, a cloud service proving application uptime monitoring and real-time notifications, suffered an outage when two of its three independent cloud deployments in different data centers began experiencing connectivity issues and high network latency. It was found later that the two independent deployments shared a common peering point which was experiencing network instability. While the third deployment was still operational, PagerDuty's applications failed to establish quorum due to to high network latency and hence failed in their ability to send notifications.

# PM-087 — GitHub

**Company:** GitHub  
**Category:** Database  
**Source:** https://github.blog/2022-03-23-an-update-on-recent-service-disruptions/

## Incident Summary

Peak-hour load on the shared `mysql1` cluster repeatedly exhausted ProxySQL connections over a week, requiring four primary failovers plus an emergency index and proactive throttling of webhooks and Actions. Memory profiling turned on to debug performance later triggered another connection failure, requiring yet another failover.

root_causes.description: Downstream dependency (partner-crm-api) experiencing performance degradation, causing webhook POST requests to block the notification-worker consumer loop. The 6200-7100ms response times (12-14x slower than expected <500ms SLA) are directly correlated with the consumer throughput collapse from 800 msg/s to 60 msg/s, indicating the worker is spending most of its time waiting for webhook responses rather than processing messages.
evidence: Webhook POST to partner-crm-api took 6200ms at 10:12:00 (expected <500ms) - 12x SLA violation
Webhook POST to partner-crm-api took 7100ms at 10:12:30 - sustained degradation
Consumer throughput dropped from 800 msg/s to 60 msg/s at 10:13:10 - 93% reduction correlates with webhook latency spike
No errors in consumer loop, no rebalances, no resource exhaustion - rules out internal consumer issues, points to blocking I/O on external dependency

root_causes.description: Kafka broker disk usage spike (68% → 71%) may indicate broker-side performance degradation or compaction activity, reducing broker throughput and increasing message processing latency. Combined with the webhook latency, this creates a dual bottleneck where messages are both slow to be consumed and slow to be delivered.
evidence: Disk usage on kafka-broker-3 increased from 68% baseline to 71% at 10:13:00
Disk usage spike coincides with consumer throughput collapse (within 3 minutes)
RB-019 identifies consumer processing time increases as a root cause of lag spikes; broker-side disk I/O contention could slow message retrieval
PM-087 (GitHub) demonstrates how resource exhaustion on shared infrastructure (database connections) can cascade to cause throughput collapse similar to observed pattern

root_causes.description: Partner-crm-api endpoint may be rate-limiting or throttling the notification-worker service (429 responses or connection pooling exhaustion), causing the worker to back off or queue requests, which blocks the consumer loop and prevents message processing. This aligns with RB-010 webhook delivery failure patterns.
evidence: RB-010 identifies target rate-limiting (429 responses) as a possible cause of webhook delivery failures and latency
Sustained 6200-7100ms response times suggest either rate-limit backoff or connection pool exhaustion at partner-crm-api
Consumer throughput dropped to 60 msg/s (7.5% of baseline) - consistent with severe rate-limiting or connection starvation
No errors logged in consumer loop suggests requests are succeeding but slowly, not failing - typical of rate-limit or connection pool scenarios

contributing_factors: Synchronous webhook delivery architecture: notification-worker blocks on each webhook POST, making it vulnerable to downstream latency. Async/queue-based delivery would decouple the consumer from webhook performance.
Lack of webhook timeout or circuit breaker: no evidence of request timeouts or fallback behavior when partner-crm-api is slow, allowing requests to block indefinitely.
Kafka broker disk usage at 71% approaching typical warning threshold (75-80%), reducing broker performance headroom and potentially triggering compaction or throttling.
No upstream monitoring/alerting on partner-crm-api latency: the webhook latency spike was only detected via notification-worker logs, not via proactive partner API health checks.
Single partition or limited parallelism in webhook delivery: if webhooks are processed serially per message, a single slow endpoint blocks all downstream message processing.

# RB-019 — Kafka Consumer Lag Spike

**Service:** Kafka / Message Queue  
**Severity:** High

**Company:** Tarsnap  
**Category:** Uncategorized

## Incident Summary

A batch job which scans for unused blocks in Amazon S3 and marks them to be freed encountered a condition where all retries for freeing certain blocks would fail. root_causes.description: Memory leak in image-resizer application causing monotonic RSS growth independent of request rate
evidence: RSS climbed steadily from 180MB to 1.9GB over ~2 hours (09:00 to 11:45) while request rate remained constant at ~40 req/s
RB-008 describes identical symptom pattern: 'RSS memory of Python process grows monotonically over hours/days' and 'Memory doesn't drop after periods of low traffic'
Pod restarted cleanly with RSS reset to 180MB, indicating no external load change but internal memory accumulation
No deploys in last 6 hours rules out recent code changes as trigger, suggesting pre-existing leak in current version

root_causes.description: Memory limit set too low relative to actual steady-state memory requirements of the service
evidence: Container memory limit is 2GB but pod reached 1.9GB at only ~40 req/s request rate

No traffic spike observed (request rate unchanged), indicating the limit itself is the constraint rather than temporary overload

root_causes.description: Upstream dependency (e.g., image storage service or cache backend) returning larger payloads or accumulating data in memory without proper cleanup
evidence: PM-098 (Amazon) demonstrates how latent memory leak in a reporting agent was triggered by inability to contact a downstream service, causing unbounded memory consumption
image-resizer likely depends on image storage/retrieval services; if a dependency began returning larger responses or caching behavior changed, it could cause memory accumulation
Steady request rate with climbing memory suggests data is being retained rather than processed and released
No recent deploys to image-resizer itself, but upstream service changes are not ruled out

contributing_factors: Container memory limit of 2GB may be insufficient for the service's normal operational requirements, leaving no headroom for temporary spikes or memory fragmentation
Lack of memory monitoring/alerting before OOMKill threshold—no proactive alerts fired when RSS crossed 1GB or 1.5GB to allow manual intervention
Pod restart by Kubernetes was automatic (exit code 137 = OOMKilled), masking the underlying leak and allowing it to recur on next restart cycle
No graceful degradation or circuit-breaker logic to shed load or clear caches when memory pressure is detected, similar to the failure mode in PM-098
Possible unbounded caching or buffering of image data in memory without TTL or size limits, exacerbated by steady request rate over extended period

# RB-012 — Node Disk Pressure

**Service:** Kubernetes Node  
**Severity:** High  
**Tags:** kubernetes, disk, storage, node, eviction

## Symptoms
- `kubectl get nodes` shows `DiskPressure=True` condition
- Pods begin getting evicted (`kubectl get events | grep Evicted`)

## Incident Summary

On September 8th, 2022, our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. root_causes.description: cert-manager ACME challenge timeout during certificate renewal, causing automatic renewal to fail silently without alerting. The certificate expired 6 days later when the 24-hour retry window passed without successful renewal, resulting in all SSL handshakes failing for clients connecting to public-api.
evidence: Log entry at 00:00:00: 'cert-manager: certificate public-api-tls renewal attempt failed, ACME challenge timeout'
Log entry at 00:00:05: 'cert-manager: will retry renewal in 24h (no alert configured on renewal failure)' - indicates silent failure with no monitoring
Log entry at 06:00:00: 'certificate public-api-tls expired 2 hours ago (6 days after the failed renewal attempt above)' - confirms 6-day gap between failed renewal and expiry

RB-017 lists 'DNS validation failing for Let's Encrypt renewal challenge' and 'Auto-renewal (Let's Encrypt / cert-manager) failed silently' as documented possible causes for this exact symptom pattern

root_causes.description: Missing or inadequate alerting on cert-manager renewal failures allowed the failed renewal at 00:00:00 to go unnoticed for 6 days until the certificate actually expired. The explicit log message 'no alert configured on renewal failure' indicates a monitoring gap that prevented timely remediation.
evidence: Log entry explicitly states: 'no alert configured on renewal failure'
6-day gap between failed renewal attempt (00:00:00) and certificate expiry (06:00:00 6 days later) with no intervention
RB-017 symptom list includes certificate expiry scenarios, implying this is a known failure mode that should be monitored
The incident was only detected when clients began receiving SSL handshake errors, indicating reactive rather than proactive detection

root_causes.description: DNS validation failure during ACME challenge (upstream dependency issue). The ACME challenge timeout suggests the Let's Encrypt validation mechanism could not complete, likely due to DNS resolution issues preventing the ACME server from validating domain ownership.
evidence: Log entry: 'ACME challenge timeout' indicates the challenge validation did not complete within expected timeframe
RB-017 explicitly lists 'DNS validation failing for Let's Encrypt renewal challenge' as a possible cause
PM-041 (rust-lang) demonstrates a real-world precedent where DNS issues during certificate operations caused service disruption
ACME challenges require DNS resolution to succeed; timeout suggests upstream DNS dependency failure rather than cert-manager misconfiguration

contributing_factors: No alert configured on cert-manager renewal failure - allowed the issue to persist undetected for 6 days
24-hour retry window in cert-manager was insufficient given the 6-day gap to expiry - should have had more aggressive retry or escalation
Traffic and deploy history unremarkable in last 7 days - no recent changes that might have triggered investigation or monitoring review
Lack of pre-expiry certificate monitoring - no alert triggered when certificate entered warning period (e.g., 30 days before expiry)
Single certificate renewal path with no fallback mechanism - no secondary renewal method or manual override process documented

## Incident Summary

Multiple SimpleDB storage nodes lost power simultaneously in one US-East data center. # PM-156 — Google

**Company:** Google  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://status.cloud.google.com/incidents/ow5i3PPK96RduMcb1SsW

## Incident Summary

A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, Google's API management and control plane system. The code path that failed was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling. When the policy data replicated globally, it caused Service Control binaries to crash loop across all regions. While a red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, larger regions like us-central-1 experienced extended recovery times (up to 2h 40m) due to a thundering herd problem when Service Control tasks restarted, overloading the underlying Spanner infrastructure. The incident affected Google and Google Cloud APIs globally, with recovery times varying by product architecture.

root_causes.description: Upstream Stripe API degradation causing cascading thread pool exhaustion in billing-service. Stripe's elevated error rates and p99 latency spike (4500ms vs expected <400ms) triggered exponential retry behavior (3.2 avg retries vs 0.1 baseline), consuming all available worker threads in billing-service's thread pool and rendering it unable to accept new requests.
evidence: Stripe status page reports 'investigating elevated error rates' at 12:00:30, preceding thread pool exhaustion at 12:01:30
Stripe API p99 latency 4500ms (expected <400ms) - 11x increase in latency
Retry count per request climbing to avg 3.2 (baseline 0.1) - 32x increase in retry rate
Thread pool exhaustion error at 12:01:30: 'thread pool exhausted, 0 available workers for new requests'

root_causes.description: Insufficient thread pool sizing or lack of circuit breaker / timeout configuration in billing-service when calling Stripe API. Without proper backpressure mechanisms, slow Stripe responses caused threads to accumulate and block, preventing the service from shedding load or failing fast.
evidence: PM-124 (CircleCI) demonstrates how JVM thread pool shrinkage masked underlying capacity problems and caused cascading outages
PM-170 (incident.io) shows connection pool tuning was critical to handling upstream latency spikes
RB-006 lists 'Resource exhaustion: thread pool saturation' as a possible cause of 5xx errors
No evidence of circuit breaker or timeout preventing threads from blocking on slow Stripe calls

root_causes.description: Retry storm amplification due to aggressive retry logic without exponential backoff or jitter. As Stripe degraded, billing-service's retry mechanism (3.2x baseline) created a feedback loop that further congested both the Stripe API and billing-service's own thread pool.
evidence: Retry count per request climbing to avg 3.2 (baseline 0.1) indicates aggressive retry behavior
PM-102 (Amazon) demonstrates how retry storms can create persistent congestion and amplify latency
Stripe status page shows 'investigating elevated error rates' - retries from billing-service likely contributed to Stripe's load
No evidence of exponential backoff or jitter in retry configuration

contributing_factors: Lack of circuit breaker pattern to fail fast and prevent thread accumulation when Stripe API latency exceeded acceptable thresholds
No timeout configuration on Stripe API calls, allowing threads to block indefinitely waiting for slow responses
Aggressive retry logic without exponential backoff or jitter, amplifying load on already-degraded Stripe API
Insufficient monitoring/alerting on upstream dependency latency (Stripe p99 latency) to trigger preventive action before thread pool exhaustion
No rate limiting or request queuing to shed load gracefully when billing-service thread pool approaches saturation

## Incident Summary

Slow queries on the MongoDB replica sets backing the build queue caused workflows to back up over a two-week run of incidents. **Company:** Amazon

## Incident Summary

At 7:30 AM PST, an automated activity to scale capacity of one of the AWS services hosted in the main AWS network triggered an unexpected behavior from a large number of clients inside the internal network. This resulted in a large surge of connection activity that overwhelmed the networking devices between the internal network and the main AWS network, resulting in delays for communication between these networks. root_causes.description: API key rotation at warehouse-api (07:00:00) was not synchronized with credential refresh in inventory-sync's secret client. The secret client caches credentials in-memory with no TTL or refresh mechanism, causing it to continue using the old (now-invalid) API key after rotation, resulting in 401 Unauthorized errors.
evidence: API key for warehouse-api rotated at 07:00:00 per scheduled quarterly rotation
100% of warehouse-api calls failing with 401 Unauthorized starting at 07:00:10 (10 seconds after rotation)
Secret client caches credentials in-memory with no TTL/refresh, last loaded at process start 3 days ago
No code deploy in last 5 days rules out recent changes; timing correlation between key rotation and failure is definitive

root_causes.description: Lack of credential refresh mechanism in the secret client creates a brittle architecture where any upstream credential rotation immediately breaks downstream service calls. This is a design flaw rather than an operational error.
evidence: Secret client explicitly documented as caching credentials in-memory with no TTL/refresh
Credentials last loaded at process start 3 days ago, indicating no refresh has occurred since deployment
Scheduled quarterly rotation of warehouse-api key is a normal operational event that should not cause outages
Similar credential management issues cited in PM-123 (CircleCI) where key cycling required manual intervention across dependent services

contributing_factors: No alerting or monitoring on credential age/staleness in the secret client to warn of potential rotation misalignment
Lack of graceful degradation or retry logic with credential refresh on 401 errors
No coordination mechanism between secrets-vault (which rotates keys) and inventory-sync (which consumes them)
Process running for 3 days without restart, allowing stale credentials to persist in memory
Quarterly key rotation schedule not aligned with credential refresh strategy in dependent services

# PM-167 — Honeycomb

**Company:** Honeycomb  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://www.honeycomb.io/blog/incident-review-shepherd-cache-delays/

## Incident Summary

On September 8th, 2022, our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. We will first cover the background behind the incident with a high-level view of the relevant architecture, how we tried to investigate and fix the system, and finally, we’ll go over some meaningful elements that surfaced from our incident review process.

root_causes.description: Missing index on the `region` column added in v2.3.0 migration. The /reports/by-region query now performs a sequential scan on the 4.2M-row orders table instead of using an index, causing query latency to spike from <200ms to 9000ms.
evidence: Incident logs show migration adding `region` column deployed at 10:00:00, with slow queries appearing at 10:05:00
Query plan shows Seq Scan on orders (4.2M rows) for /reports/by-region endpoint

RB-002 identifies 'New code path introduced a query without a covering index' and 'Table grew past threshold where sequential scan became slower than index scan' as primary causes of slow queries with Seq Scan patterns
Expected latency <200ms vs actual 9000ms is consistent with full table scan on 4.2M rows

root_causes.description: Database statistics became stale after the migration, causing the query planner to choose a sequential scan instead of an index-based plan. The planner lacks accurate row count or selectivity estimates for the new `region` column.
evidence: PM-083 (CircleCI incident) documents identical symptom: 'post-cutover database was running every query against disk because its statistics tables had not been updated' and 'planner without usable indexes after the cutover'
Incident shows CPU and memory normal but query plan shows Seq Scan, suggesting planner miscalculation rather than resource exhaustion
Migration at 10:00:00 likely did not include ANALYZE step to refresh statistics on the modified orders table

root_causes.description: The migration script may have failed to create an index on the `region` column as part of the schema change, or the index creation was deferred and not yet executed in the deployment pipeline.
evidence: RB-023 lists 'Migration script has a bug' and 'Partial migration applied — database in inconsistent state' as possible causes
Incident description states migration 'adding `region` column' but does not explicitly confirm index creation
Timing correlation: deploy at 10:00:00 with slow queries at 10:05:00 suggests schema change incomplete or index not yet built

contributing_factors: Large table size (4.2M rows in orders table) amplifies the impact of a missing index, making sequential scans prohibitively slow
No pre-deployment performance testing or query plan validation for the /reports/by-region endpoint after the schema migration
Lack of automated index creation as part of the migration definition (index should be created alongside the new column)

# RB-002 — PostgreSQL Slow Queries and Missing Indexes

**Service:** PostgreSQL  
**Severity:** Medium–High  
**Tags:** database, postgres, performance, indexes, query-plan

## Symptoms
- API p99 latency spikes correlate with DB query duration
- `pg_stat_statements` shows queries with `mean_exec_time > 1000ms`
- `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` output shows `Seq Scan` on large tables
- Application logs contain `slow query` warnings

## Possible Causes
- New code path introduced a query without a covering index
- Table grew past threshold where sequential scan became slower than index scan
- Statistics out of date — planner choosing wrong execution plan
- `N+1` query pattern in ORM code
- Index bloat after high-churn delete/update workload

## Diagnostic Steps

```sql
-- Top 10 slowest queries (requires pg_stat_statements)
SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time, total_exec_time
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Find tables with sequential scans on large row counts
SELECT schemaname, relname, seq_scan, seq_tup_read, idx_scan
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE seq_scan > 0 AND n_live_tup > 10000
ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC;

-- Identify missing indexes via pg_missing_indexes

# PM-092 — incident.io

**Company:** incident.io  
**Category:** Database  
**Source:** https://incident.io/blog/database-performance

## Incident Summary

Two weeks of intermittent app timeouts, with traces showing requests waiting up to 20s for a connection from Go's `database/sql` pool, but contention spread across many endpoints rather than a single slow query. **Category:** Database

## Incident Summary

A blue/green upgrade of the workflows database succeeded mechanically, but the post-cutover database was running every query against disk because its statistics tables had not been updated. # RB-020 — Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) Accumulation

**Service:** Message Queue / Task Worker  
**Severity:** Medium  
**Tags:** dlq, messaging, errors, worker, retries

## Symptoms
- DLQ depth growing steadily: `redis_list_length{key="dlq:incidents"} > 100`
- Tasks that should complete are silently failing and ending up in DLQ
- Application events are lost — users notice missing notifications or delayed state
- Alert fires on `dlq_depth > threshold`

## Possible Causes
- Unhandled exception in worker code causes task to be sent to DLQ after max retries
- Deserialization failure: message format changed but workers not updated
- External dependency consistently unavailable for duration of retry window
- Task payload too large for the worker to process within timeout
- Misconfigured retry policy: max retries = 0, everything goes to DLQ immediately

## Diagnostic Steps

```bash
# Check DLQ depth
redis-cli LLEN dlq:incidents
redis-cli LRANGE dlq:incidents 0 4  # peek at first 5 messages

# Check worker error logs
kubectl logs -l app=rootcause-worker --tail=500 | grep -i 'error\|dlq\|retry\|failed'

# Check Celery (if applicable) failed tasks
celery -A rootcause inspect reserved
celery -A r

# PM-143 — Foursquare

**Company:** Foursquare  
**Category:** Uncategorized  
**Source:** https://web.archive.org/web/20230602082218/https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=1769761

## Incident Summary

MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory. The failure was catastrophic and not graceful due to a a query pattern that involved a read-load with low levels of locality (each user check-in caused a read of all check-ins for the user's history, and records were 300 bytes with no spatial locality, meaning that most of the data pulled in from each page was unnecessary). root_causes.description: Lack of audit trail and change control process for manual feature flag overrides in the admin UI. root_causes.description: Insufficient pre-production testing and validation of new_checkout_flow at scale. RB-022 identifies 'New code has a bug only triggered by a subset of production traffic' as a possible cause for canary failures
The feature passed at 5% rollout but failed at 100%, suggesting scale-dependent issues (resource exhaustion, concurrency bugs, or downstream dependency limits)

contributing_factors: No automated validation or drift detection between admin UI state and config repository state to alert on divergence
Lack of approval workflow or change control process for manual feature flag overrides in production
Feature flag system did not enforce immutability or require git-based changes for production rollout modifications
Insufficient load testing and canary validation before expanding rollout from 5% to higher percentages
No automated rollback trigger when error rate exceeded acceptable thresholds (6% error rate should have triggered automatic rollback per RB-022 guidance)

**Category:** Uncategorized

## Incident Summary

A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, Google's API management and control plane system. The code path that failed was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling. When the policy data replicated globally, it caused Service Control binaries to crash loop across all regions. # RB-016 — DNS Resolution Failures

**Service:** DNS / Service Discovery  
**Severity:** Critical  
**Tags:** dns, networking, service-discovery, coreDNS

## Symptoms
- `socket.gaierror: [Errno -2] Name or service not known`
- `ConnectionError: HTTPSConnectionPool ... Max retries exceeded`
- Services unable to reach each other by hostname within cluster
- External API calls failing with DNS-level errors
- `nslookup` / `dig` returning `SERVFAIL` or `NXDOMAIN`

## Possible Causes
- CoreDNS pods crashing or OOMKilled in Kubernetes
- DNS search domain misconfiguration (`resolv.conf`)
- Split-horizon DNS issue (internal vs external resolution conflict)
- ndots configuration causing excessive DNS lookups for short names
- DNS cache poisoned or stale
- Network policy blocking UDP port 53 to CoreDNS

## Diagnostic Steps

```bash
# Test DNS from inside a pod
kubectl run -it --rm dns-test --image=busybox --restart=Never -- \
  nslookup kubernetes.default

# Check CoreDNS pod status
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Check CoreDNS logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns --tail=100

# Check resolv.conf inside affected pod
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- cat /etc

root_causes.description: HPA metric misconfiguration: queue_depth metric exporter was renamed during monitoring migration, but HPA configuration still references the old metric name. This causes the HPA to receive a metric value of 0 instead of the actual queue depth, preventing autoscaling from triggering despite queue backing up to 9000 items.
evidence: Log entry 17:08:00: 'queue_depth metric exporter was renamed during last week's monitoring migration; HPA config still references the old metric name'
Log entry 17:06:00: 'target metric `external.queue_depth` reporting value 0 consistently for the last hour'
Log entry 17:00:00: 'queue depth climbing, 200 -> 9000 over 25 minutes' while log 17:05:00 shows 'current replicas 4, desired replicas 4 (no change)'
RB-015 lists 'HPA target metric name wrong or metrics API not returning data' as a possible cause for autoscaling not triggering under load

root_causes.description: Upstream dependency degradation or failure causing checkout-worker tasks to fail or back up in the queue. While CPU/memory per-pod are normal at ~40% utilization, the queue is accumulating faster than workers can process, suggesting either workers are blocked waiting on a downstream service or tasks are being rejected/retried.
evidence: Queue depth climbing from 200 to 9000 in 25 minutes while per-pod CPU/memory remain at ~40% utilization suggests workers are not CPU-bound but are unable to process tasks at normal rate
RB-020 describes DLQ accumulation scenarios where 'External dependency consistently unavailable for duration of retry window' causes queue buildup

PM-163 (Heroku incident) describes a similar pattern: 'dependency upgrade that caused a timing lock issue that greatly reduced the throughput of our task workers' coupled with 'failure to appropriately scale up due to increased workload scheduling, caused the application's work queue to build up'

root_causes.description: Monitoring migration incomplete or broken: the metric exporter rename was not accompanied by corresponding HPA configuration updates, indicating a process failure in the monitoring migration that occurred last week. This is a configuration management issue rather than a runtime failure.
evidence: Log entry 17:08:00 explicitly states: 'queue_depth metric exporter was renamed during last week's monitoring migration; HPA config still references the old metric name'
The timing aligns with 'last week's monitoring migration' suggesting the root cause was introduced during that change window
RB-015 identifies 'HPA target metric name wrong' as a known cause of autoscaling failures, and this appears to be exactly what occurred

contributing_factors: Lack of validation/testing after monitoring migration: the metric rename was not validated against HPA configuration before deployment
No alerting on HPA metric health: the fact that `external.queue_depth` was reporting 0 for an hour without triggering an alert suggests missing observability on metric validity
Low initial replica count (minReplicas=4): even if autoscaling had triggered, the baseline capacity was already insufficient for the incoming load
Delayed incident detection: queue depth climbed for 25 minutes before being logged as a warning, suggesting insufficient real-time alerting on queue depth thresholds

Normal per-pod resource utilization masked the problem: CPU/memory at ~40% meant traditional resource-based autoscaling would not have triggered, making the external metric the only viable scaling signal

# RB-015 — Autoscaling Not Triggering Under Load

**Service:** Kubernetes HPA  
**Severity:** High  
**Tags:** kubernetes, hpa, autoscaling, capacity

## Symptoms
- CPU/memory utilization high but pod count stays at `minReplicas`
- HPA shows `<unknown>` for current metrics
- `kubectl describe hpa` shows `unable to get metrics` or `failed to compute desired replicas`
- Application latency rises under load with no scale-out

## Possible Causes
- `metrics-server` not installed or not running in the cluster
- CPU `requests` not set on the deployment (HPA requires requests to compute utilization %)
- HPA target metric name wrong or metrics API not returning data
- `minReplicas == maxReplicas` (effectively disabled)
- Scale-down cooldown preventing scale-up due to a bug in HPA configuration
- Custom metric adapter (Prometheus Adapter) not configured correctly

## Diagnostic Steps

```bash
# Check HPA status
kubectl describe hpa rootcause-api-hpa

# Check if metrics-server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server

# Check if metrics API is available
kubectl top pods -l app=rootcause-api

# Check if CPU requests are set
kubectl get deployment rootcause-api \
  -o js

# RB-020 — Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) Accumulation

**Service:** Message Queue / Task Worker

- Deserialization failure: message format changed but workers not updated

root_causes.description: Read-write consistency violation due to excessive replication lag on postgres-replica-2. Users' writes are committed successfully on postgres-primary, but reads are routed to postgres-replica-2 which is lagging 45 seconds behind (baseline <1s). This causes users to read stale data immediately after writing, creating the illusion that their updates didn't save.
evidence: Replication lag on postgres-replica-2 is 45 seconds vs baseline <1s (log 12:02:00)
Writes confirmed committed on postgres-primary with normal throughput and latency (log 12:03:00)
Reads for profile endpoint explicitly routed to postgres-replica-2 for load balancing (log 12:01:00)
User symptom: 'profile update didn't save' despite UI confirming success indicates write succeeded but read returned stale data

root_causes.description: Heavy I/O contention from nightly ETL export job running against postgres-replica-2 is causing replication lag. The large export job started at 11:50 and is consuming heavy I/O resources, preventing the replica from keeping up with write replication from the primary.
evidence: Large nightly export job running against postgres-replica-2 since 11:50 consuming heavy I/O (log 12:04:00)
Replication lag spike to 45 seconds coincides with ETL job execution window (logs 12:02:00-12:04:00)
postgres-primary shows normal write throughput and latency, indicating the problem is replica-side resource contention (log 12:03:00)
PM-151 (Gitlab) demonstrates how heavy workloads can cause replication lag leading to data consistency issues

contributing_factors: Read-heavy load balancing strategy routes all profile reads to a single replica (postgres-replica-2) rather than distributing across multiple replicas or using read-after-write consistency
Lack of read-after-write consistency guarantees in the application layer - no mechanism to route post-write reads back to primary or wait for replication
ETL export job scheduled during peak hours or without resource isolation/throttling to prevent I/O contention with replication
Insufficient monitoring/alerting on replication lag thresholds - 45 second lag should have triggered automated mitigation before user impact
No circuit breaker or fallback mechanism to detect high replication lag and redirect reads to primary when replica falls behind
